title: 2 Kings 4
subtitle: Elisha’s Miracles — The Prophet Among the People

heb.paragraph: The widow has only an ʾasûkh shemen — a small flask of oil (v.2). The same word appears in 1 Kgs 17:12 for the widow of Zarephath’s jar. Elijah and Elisha both multiply the last reserves of destitute widows — the prophetic ministry consistently begins with the marginalised.

refs.note: The widow of Zarephath’s oil never ran out. Elijah sustained one widow; Elisha multiplies oil to save another. The pattern escalates.
The widow’s mite — another destitute widow whose tiny resource God honours disproportionately.
Sabbatical year debt release was designed to prevent this situation. The creditor’s cruelty violates covenant social legislation.

notes.note: God asks what we have, not what we lack. He begins with our resources and multiplies them. The principle governs all ministry. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: ʾasûkh shāmen — a narrow-mouthed jug, probably holding a few ounces. The diminutive size emphasises the contrast with the resulting abundance. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Debt slavery was regulated by both Israelite law (Exod 21:2–4; Lev 25:39–42) and Mesopotamian law codes (Hammurabi §§117–119). The six-year limit was often ignored during economic stress.
Olive oil was the primary liquid commodity — used for cooking, lighting, medicine, cosmetics, and ritual. A supply filling many vessels would represent substantial wealth.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The widow addresses Elisha as the successor responsible for the prophetic community. The master is responsible for deceased prophets’ dependents.
The cessation of oil when vessels run out introduces a theology of human participation in miracle. God works within the space human faith creates.

hist.context: A prophet’s widow faces a creditor who will take her two sons as debt slaves. She has nothing but a flask of oil. Elisha tells her to borrow empty vessels, then pour. The oil flows until every vessel is full. She sells the oil, pays the debt, and lives on the rest. The miracle addresses real-world need: debt slavery was a concrete social crisis. God’s provision works through human action (she must borrow), is proportional to faith (the oil stops when vessels run out), and is comprehensive (debt paid, sons freed, livelihood secured).

notes.note: The narrative of Kings presents Israel's history as a theological argument: obedience to God's covenant brings blessing; disobedience brings judgment. This section continues that theme, demonstrating that God's sovereignty operates through human choices and their consequences. Every passage in Kings ultimately points to the need for a perfect King who will fulfill what no earthly monarch could.

The theological framework of Kings evaluates every monarch by a single standard: fidelity to the covenant expressed in exclusive worship of Yahweh at the chosen place. Political success, military prowess, and economic prosperity are irrelevant apart from covenant obedience. This standard anticipates the NT principle that faithfulness to God's Word is the only measure of true success (Rev 2:10).

The practical lesson for every believer is that obedience to God's Word is the only path to genuine blessing. The kings who prospered spiritually were those who feared the Lord and walked in His ways. The same principle governs the Christian life: 'If you love Me, keep My commandments' (John 14:15). Obedience is not the means of salvation but the evidence of it.

sections.header: Verses 8–37 — The Shunammite Woman; Her Son Raised from Death

heb.paragraph: The Shunammite builds Elisha an ʿaliyyat qîr (v.10) — a permanent roofed room with bed, table, chair, and lamp. She creates institutional infrastructure for the prophetic ministry.
The verb hāvĕq frames the miracle: the child she was promised (“you will embrace a son,” v.16), then held in death (v.20), she now holds alive (v.37).

refs.note: Elijah raised the widow of Zarephath’s son by stretching over the child three times. Elisha uses the same method (v.34).
Jesus raises the widow of Nain’s son. The crowd declares, “A great prophet has appeared.” The Elijah-Elisha echo is deliberate.
Sarah laughed at the promise of a son; the Shunammite resists for the same reason. Both receive against all expectation.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: ʿaliyyat qîr qĕtannāh — “a small upper room on the wall.” A permanent structure with bed, table, chair, lamp — a fully equipped guest room. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

wayyasem pîw ʿal-pîw — “he placed his mouth on his mouth.” The parallel with 1 Kgs 17:21 is exact. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Shunem (modern Solem) was on the Hill of Moreh in the Jezreel Valley, about 10 km south of the Sea of Galilee, on the north-south route — a natural stopping point for travellers.
The “stretching upon the child” parallels 1 Kings 17:21. The biblical narrative frames it as prophetic intercession, not sympathetic magic.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: “Did I ask you for a son?” is the book’s most piercing critique of prophetic promise. Her grief challenges Elisha to demonstrate that God’s prophets are agents of resurrection, not dispensers of empty words.
Gehazi’s staff fails; Elisha’s presence succeeds. Prophetic power is not transferable through objects. There are no shortcuts in the ministry of resurrection.

hist.context: The longest narrative in the Elisha cycle. A wealthy Shunammite builds Elisha a room, and he promises her a son. The child is born but dies years later of apparent heatstroke. She rides to Carmel: “Did I ask you for a son? Didn’t I say, Don’t raise my hopes?” Gehazi’s staff fails; Elisha comes himself, lies upon the child, and the boy revives. The miracle parallels 1 Kings 17:17–24 but with deeper emotional texture — particularly the woman’s resistance to hope and her raw grief when hope fails.

notes.note: The formulaic record of this king's reign and death reminds us that every earthly throne is temporary. No matter how powerful or prosperous, every human ruler faces the same end. Only the throne of David's greater Son endures forever (Luke 1:32-33). The believer's hope rests not in human leadership but in Christ's eternal kingdom.

The recurring refrain of royal death and succession is a sobering reminder of human mortality. Yet for the believer, death has lost its sting through the resurrection of Christ (1 Cor 15:55-57). The hope of the Old Testament saints — dimly perceived but genuinely held — has been fully revealed in the gospel: those who trust in Christ will reign with Him forever (2 Tim 2:12; Rev 22:5).

sections.header: Verses 38–44 — Death in the Pot; Feeding a Hundred

refs.note: Jesus feeds 5,000 with five loaves and two fish — leftovers fill twelve baskets. Elisha feeds 100 with twenty loaves. The pattern is identical; the scale transforms.
“They all ate and were satisfied.” Jesus’ miracle exceeds Elisha’s by a factor of fifty.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: lehem bikkûrîm — “bread of firstfruits.” Normally brought to the sanctuary (Deut 26:1–11). Bringing them to Elisha indicates the prophet was functioning as an alternative religious authority. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The “wild gourds” (paqqûʿot) are likely Citrullus colocynthis, the colocynth or “bitter apple,” common in the Jordan Valley — highly purgative and potentially lethal.

Baal-Shalishah (modern Kefr Thilth) was about 25 km north of Lydda. The man’s gift of firstfruits to Elisha rather than a sanctuary suggests the prophetic community was a recognised recipient of sacral offerings.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The servant sees twenty loaves and a hundred mouths. Elisha sees the word of the LORD. Obedience to the prophetic word, not mathematics, determines the outcome. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: Two brief miracles close the chapter, both during famine. Poisonous gourds contaminate the prophets’ stew; Elisha adds flour and it becomes safe. Then a man brings twenty barley loaves and Elisha feeds a hundred prophets with leftovers. Both foreshadow Jesus: the healing of contaminated food recalls his authority over clean and unclean (Mark 7:18–19); the feeding of a hundred with twenty loaves anticipates the feeding of the five thousand (John 6). The ratio is 1:5; Jesus’ miracle is 1:1,000. Elisha establishes the category; Jesus shatters the scale.

heb.paragraph: The nāḇîʾ (prophet) in Chronicles functions as God's spokesperson — the voice that interprets events theologically. The Chronicler adds prophetic interventions to narratives where Kings has none, ensuring that every crisis has a divine commentary. Prophets do not predict the future so much as explain the present in light of God's covenant purposes.

ppl.text: God’s provision works through human action (she must borrow), is proportional to faith (the oil stops when vessels run out), and is comprehensive (debt paid, sons freed, livelihood secured)…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVShe said to her husband, “I know that this man who often comes our way is a holy man of God.ESVAnd she said to her husband, “Behold now, I know that this is a holy man of God who is continually passing our way.NIVOne day Elisha went to Shunem. And a well-to-do woman was there, who urged him to stay for a meal. So whenever he came by, he stopped there to eat.ESVOne day Elisha went on to Shunem, where a wealthy woman lived, who urged him to eat some food. So whenever he passed that way, he would turn in there to eat food.

themes.note: Elisha’s Miracles — The Prophet Among the People

coaching.tip: The widow's oil multiplies until she runs out of vessels. The miracle is limited not by God's supply but by human preparation — she should have borrowed more jars. The principle applies broadly: God's provision often scales to the size of our expectation and effort.

prayer_prompt: As you reflect on Elisha's miracles among ordinary people — oil multiplied, a child raised, poison neutralized — consider asking God whether you believe He cares about your everyday crises as much as the dramatic ones.
